speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five for Bernard Schuster. The date is December 28, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my dear Aunt Mary [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] said she would take me to a doctor. Because I had lost my mind. I'm dreaming of becoming a lawyer. But I don't even speak any English. And I'm already 18 years old. And she said children who are born here who speak the language, it takes them four years of high school, four years of college, and then law school. She says you'll be a grandfather before you finish college.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you talking about? I said, well, maybe so. But I said, give me a week. Let me find a job. And let me find a room someplace. And let me give it a try. Nothing lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the neighborhood, there lived a lawyer on Berkeley Street near Canterbury by the name of Rubin Goldman. And Rubin spoke very good Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, he's got a son who's a lawyer here, Arnold Goldman. And so I went to Rubin. And I told him what my problems were, that I needed a job, and I needed a place to stay. He said, well, I can't help you with a place to stay. But I can help you with a job. He called a friend of his, Noah Scher. Who owned a chain of bicycle stores and toys, Noah's Ark. They had a warehouse on Railroad Street off Main Street not far from East Evening High School, which was on Alexander Street, corner of Alexander and Main. And so Rubin Goldman called Noah Scher. And Noah said, sure, I'll give him a job. So they gave me a job. My job was to work nine hours a day loading and unloading bicycles mostly, which were in crates, crated bicycles. And I signed up at night school, East Evening High School. And there I met a wonderful human being by the name of Carlos Desafra. I have a picture here someplace of Carlos. He was like a father to me. He was the assistant principal, a very decent human being. And he said, look. I'll help you. And we got to know each other well. He would stay and wait up for me and take me home. I got a room way out on Alphonse Street. There was an old couple that couldn't take care of their house. In fact, the man died soon after I moved in there. But they gave me a room in return for which I was to take care of the furnace and cutting the gas and so on. And I went to East Evening. I would go in the morning to the warehouse, work nine hours, wash up, stop over at the little restaurant that is still there. Last night, my wife and I drove by to look at it, the Penguin Restaurant corner of Alexander and Main. It has been there all those years. I used to go in and look at the menu and see what I can buy for $0.59, because that's all I could afford for dinner. And then I would go to the high school. And Carlos-- I said, Carlos, I've got to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finish high school faster, in less than four years. My aunt said, I'll be a grandfather before I finish college. I said, so I want to do it fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Carlos said, look, you are bright. Let's write to Albany and see if they'll give you permission to go to three classes every hour. Instead of 45 minutes in a class, you'll be in each class 15 minutes. So I was in each class 15 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would skip from class to class to class. And in one year, I finished all four years of high school. I was short a mark in algebra, so I went to summer school that year and finished up the algebra course, and finished, and got a regent diploma, 16 credits of high school. And in fact, I have an article here-- I don't know, maybe you'd like take a shot of it-- that describes my year at high school, the fact that I lost 20 pounds that year. And- it was fun. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in '48, I-- I entered the University of Rochester. They gave me a scholarship. And that's when I slowed down. I decided I would socialize. By that time, I could speak English fairly well, well enough to take exams in-- in high school and do all right. And I came to college. I-- I had wonderful friends. There was a Jewish fraternity, Kappa Nu. And they got to know me and offered me a scholarship. Because I could not afford it. I was still working. Through college, I still worked in the warehouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave me a scholarship and helped me learn English slang, things I wouldn't learn from books. And I had a wonderful time in college. I really enjoyed myself very much. And I did very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the time I was a senior in college, I decided I would go on to law school. I should also back track and say that after I graduated from high school, my aunt came to me. And she begged me to forgive her. And we remained very close all those years until she died in-- about 15 years ago. But she was very good to me. And I appreciated everything she did. And I remained very close to her daughter, who died recently, Bernice. But she realized that I meant what I said, and I could do it. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in any event, so here I was graduating from college. And I had to go to law school. And again, I was very insecure. I had no money. I still-- my English was still not as good as I knew some of my classmates in law school would be. And so I applied to seven law schools, all the best, Harvard, Yale, Columbia, Cornell, Michigan, Chicago, and NYU. And I sat back and waited. And lo and behold, I received seven acceptances. So I said, well, wait a minute. Something is wrong. But then I wrote to all of them. And I said, look, I need a scholarship. And I sat back, and lo and behold, I got seven scholarships. The lowest one was Harvard. Cornell offered me three years, all expenses paid. Whereas, Harvard, I-- I forgot. They gave me something like $1,000 a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, again, I went to Rubin Goldman, who was a lawyer by profession. And I said, Rubin, you've given me good advice before. Which would you do? Where would you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, forget about it. 10 years from now, the difference in the few dollars-- he says borrow all the money. Go to Harvard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to Harvard. I didn't enjoy it as much as I enjoyed college. Law school was-- Harvard Law School was a tough place to be. I did quite well, well enough so that when I graduated, I got a clerkship with the Court of Appeals.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, here's a picture of the Court of Appeals that I worked far with the seven judges. I worked for Judge Dye, but also I helped out all the others. And it was a tradition there, when you finished your clerkship, all of the judges would give you a picture of the court that they would all sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I clerked for the Court of Appeals in Albany for two and a half years. Then I decided-- by that time, I got married. I came back from law school, actually, and me Jane, my wife to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got married right after I graduated from law school in 1955. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jane was a teacher at the time. She had finished college at Cornell and got a master's at the U of R and was teaching at Monroe High School. And we decided to settle down in Rochester, because Jane was a native Rochesterian, and to raise a family here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had three children, Deborah, Jaime, and Miriam. Two of them now live in Rochester. Jaime is a radiologist. And I believe his two children and my wife Jane will be coming here in a few minutes, so that you'll get a chance to meet them. Of course, you know Jaime, And you know Jane. And life has been awfully good to me. I-- I have very few regrets. I really think that life has been so terribly good to me that it's as if fate had tried to make up to me for-- [CRYING] for the setbacks. It's too bad that most of my family is not around to see it or to know about it. But at least, I had Baile and Feitche. We were very close. My sister, Feitche, kept a diary. And then she reduced it to a manuscript, which I have here, in Yiddish, which I do hope to turn over to the Holocaust Museum. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She describes that part, which I have talked about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's been-- it's been a wonderful life, really. I've had lots of joy for my children. And I have tremendous joy from my grandchildren. I've been successful professionally. I'm economically independent. I-- I have practiced law for many, many years. I've done some good. I've never had anyone complain that I know of. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've helped many people. I think I've carried out some of the goals I've set for myself. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Whatever the future holds, I will face it bravely.

speaker: interviewer
text: In light of your unique life experience, is there any message you would like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message is really quite obvious. Hitler could have been stopped many times. People-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think there's an old quote. I forgot the name of the Hungarian, I believe it was, who said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the Germans came for the gypsies, I didn't care, because I wasn't a gypsy. And when they came for the Jews, I didn't care, because I wasn't a Jew. And then they came for me. And there was no one left to cry out. And that is the message. If you allow genocide, anti-Semitism, anti-anything, if you allow prejudice and inhumanity to go on, it will produce other Holocausts. And we saw it in Bosnia-Herzegovina. We're seeing it now. I think we must speak out. I have devoted a lot of my mature years to try and prevent it. I have served on many boards, many organizations, Jewish and non-Jewish, in order to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do whatever little bit I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one should say that what they do doesn't really matter, because they're only one person. And what can one person do? It has to start someplace. It has to be started by someone. And we're all responsible. We're all our brothers' keepers. Is there anything else you'd like to include that you haven't mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think I've said it pretty much. I could go on and on and on. Hopefully, I hope to publish a book in addition to the memoirs. Now, that I'm slowing down professionally in the office, I hope to devote more time to it. I have done a fair amount of teaching on the subject. We had a professor, Cobb, Abraham Cobb, from a rabbi who was a professor at the university teaching a course on the Holocaust over a 17-year period. And I have gone in as a guest lecturer each and every time he taught the course. I've also spoken to many high schools, many synagogues, many other groups. I feel that the story must be told.

speaker: interviewer
text: In my sister's manuscript, she goes on to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] read-- to write that perhaps no one will survive. And if, per chance, someone does survive, will anyone believe? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nevertheless, the story must be told. So I'm writing it. Those were words she used in her manuscript. And that is true. Well, thank you for all your years of work, and especially for today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're photographing my mother's father, my grandfather Elie, E-L-I-E, Maretzki, M-A-R-E-T-Z-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would imagine somewhere around the turn of the century, around maybe 1905, 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was taken in a town called [INAUDIBLE], which is where he lived where my mother was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only picture I have of my parents and some of my siblings. There were eight of us in our family. In this picture, there are only five. It was taken some seven years before I was born. In the center are my mother and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah and Yacov or Jacob. Then-- and the-- what would these be-- the left side or the right side?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side is my sister, Baile. And then the next one, the young man in the middle, is my brother, Meyer, my sister, Mariashe. To the left of my mother, the taller one, is my sister Feitche. And the young one is my sister, Dverke, who was probably maybe four or five years older at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Jasionowka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since it was about seven years before I was born, I would say it was taken around 1920, '21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows a good part of my extended family in Jasionowka. Not only does it contain my mother, father, and five of their older children, but also most of-- many of my uncles and aunts and some of my cousins, who lived in Jasionowka at that time. It was taken around 1920, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows my-- in the center is my sister, Feitche. And I'm not sure whether they were married at the time. But next to her is either her husband or fiance, Avremel. Lying on the ground is my brother, Meyer, whom I loved very dearly. And he must've been, I'd say, about 18, 19 at the time. The two people on top, one is my mother's brother, Zeidke. And one is a nephew of my mother's, Yankele Kliminsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken probably, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say, in the late '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jasionowka. This picture, on the right side standing is my sister Dverke. It's the only picture that I do have of Dverke. Obviously, it was a winter scene. She's standing there with several friends, all of whom I knew, including in this picture is my cousin, Maishe, the man I've mentioned who lives in Mexico City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's in the center standing above my sister, Dverke there, the only man in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was also taken probably in the '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Jasionowka?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jasionowka.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the only picture I have of myself as a youngster. I must've been somewhere between three and four years old. I still remember the wooden horse, taken in Jasionowka, sent to my aunt in Rochester by my mother. And I found it after coming to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find the other photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister had them. And before she died, she turned them over to me. This is my brother, Maishe. He gave me this picture. And it was taken in Italy in Santa Cesarea in 1946. He now lives in Melbourne, Australia. This is my brother, Meyer with his wife, Rina. She was from Lodz. And when they first met, she did not speak any Yiddish even though she was Jewish. And she had to learn Yiddish in our family in Jasionowka where she lived. Meyer was a wonderful brother. He's the one who taught me how to play chess and read to me. And that was taken probably around 1932, '33. This is my sister, Mariashe and her husband, Chaim, one of the two brothers who married two of my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I start all over?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken also in the early '30s in Jasionowka. This is a sketch of the hiding place, which my family had built above the tannery. The tannery was a very long building, probably about 100 feet long. It was a one-story building. But when my father bought the tannery, he built a drying room above the main floor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which occupied a good part of the steeple of the attic. And thus, above the-- the drawing room was a space, probably 100 feet long, about 20 feet wide, and about 3 and 1/2, 4 feet high at the highest point. What we did is we built a blind wall in the front of the building, so that anybody-- we could get up there to the hiding place, to the crawling space, on a rope ladder at the other end of the building, crawl on our hands and knees until we got to the front of the building, open the little trapdoor, which we built from the boards that looked exactly like the wall in front. Close the door, once we were inside, so that even if somebody came looking for us, they would have to crawl 80, 90 feet on their hands and knees. And then they would come to a blind wall, which they would think was the end of the building. They were already at the front, but which really wasn't. Beyond it was about a 20 space, where some 15 or 16 of us were hiding out for two days and two nights after the town was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This article appeared, I believe, in 1948 in the local papers describing my one year of study at East Evening High School, where in one year of night classes, I finished four years of high school studies and got a regent diploma. It describes how I worked, that I lost a lot of weight, and what I achieved.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the youngest of my three children. Her name is Miriam. She's a delight. She now lives in Montana. She moved there recently from Colorado. She's a free spirit and a delightful daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken maybe five years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rochester.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son, James, who is the middle child, and his lovely wife, Jessica. Jessica has two older girls from a previous marriage and Jamie's and Jessica's two younger children, whom you will meet shortly, Sarah, who is now seven, and Saul Benjamin, who is now four. This picture was taken about three and a half years ago, I would say, judging by Saul Benjamin's age. And they're a wonderful family, wonderful. I see them every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the older kids' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older kids are [? Christina ?] and [? Laura. ?] And the younger ones, Sarah and Saul. I can hear them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest, Deborah, and her child Rachel. Rachel is now 11. In this picture, she was probably a few months old. So this picture was obviously taken about 11 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in Rochester.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of my shtetl background, I have always loved land and woods and forests. And so as soon as I could, I purchased these 56 acres, which is not far from Rochester. It is absolutely beautiful land. I had a beautiful pond dug. And my wife and I and our children and grandchildren spend a lot of time there. It's in the town of Victor. And it's just full of deer and geese and all kinds of wildlife. And we are having wonderful times. It's-- it's added a great deal to enjoyment of life for my family.
